heroOverview: Aphasia is an acquired language disorder caused by damage to the brain's language networks, most commonly the left hemisphere. It affects approximately 2 million Americans and around 250,000 new cases occur each year following stroke alone. The condition impairs the ability to speak, understand speech, read, or write, in any combination, while leaving intelligence intact. Stroke accounts for roughly 80% of cases, but traumatic brain injury, brain tumors, and neurological diseases such as primary progressive aphasia also cause it. Many people with aphasia recover meaningful language function with intensive speech-language therapy, though the degree of recovery depends on lesion size, location, and time to treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2 million people in the United States live with aphasia; about 180,000 new cases are diagnosed annually (National Aphasia Association, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults over 65 following stroke; affects men and women roughly equally
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset near 70 when stroke is the cause; younger presentations occur with traumatic brain injury or brain tumor
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 5–6 million people worldwide at any one time

definition: Aphasia (ICD-10: R47.01) is an acquired impairment of language — the ability to produce and/or comprehend spoken or written words — resulting from focal brain damage, most often to the left cerebral hemisphere where language processing is dominant in about 95% of right-handed adults. It is classified by the locus and extent of injury into several syndromic types, ranging from Broca's aphasia (halting non-fluent output with relative comprehension preservation) to Wernicke's aphasia (fluent but meaningless output with poor comprehension) and global aphasia (severe deficits in all language modalities). The underlying lesion disrupts one or more nodes of the perisylvian language network, including Broca's area (inferior frontal gyrus), Wernicke's area (posterior superior temporal gyrus), or the arcuate fasciculus connecting them. Aphasia is explicitly a language disorder, not a cognitive, speech-motor, or intellectual impairment — the person's intelligence, personality, and memories are preserved, which is a critical point for rehabilitation and for family understanding. Post-stroke aphasia is the most common acquired language disorder in adults, present in roughly 21–38% of acute stroke admissions.

typesClassification.description: Lesion in the posterior inferior frontal gyrus; speech is effortful, telegraphic, and grammatically reduced but comprehension for single words is relatively preserved. Writing mirrors the spoken deficit.

Lesion in the posterior superior temporal gyrus; output is fluent and plentiful but contains paraphasias and neologisms; auditory comprehension is markedly impaired, and the person is often unaware of errors.
Extensive perisylvian damage causing severe deficits in all language modalities — speaking, comprehension, reading, and writing; the most severe form, often following large left-hemisphere stroke.
Disruption of the arcuate fasciculus; speech is fluent with frequent phonemic paraphasias, repetition is disproportionately impaired, but comprehension is relatively spared.
Isolated word-finding difficulty with otherwise fluent, grammatically correct speech; the mildest and most common residual form after partial recovery.

A neurodegenerative syndrome causing gradual language deterioration over at least two years before other cognitive faculties are affected; caused by frontotemporal lobar degeneration or Alzheimer pathology.

primarySymptoms: Difficulty finding words (anomia) — pausing mid-sentence, using circumlocutions, or substituting an incorrect word for the intended one.
Non-fluent, effortful, or halting speech output with sentences limited to a few words, typical of frontal lesions.
Fluent but meaningless speech filled with paraphasias (substituted sounds or words) or invented words (neologisms).
Impaired comprehension of spoken language, ranging from difficulty with complex sentences to inability to follow simple commands.
Difficulty repeating words or phrases even when comprehension appears adequate.
Reading impairment (alexia) — inability to recognise written words, decode text, or extract meaning from written sentences.
Writing impairment (agraphia) — reduced writing output that mirrors spoken language deficits; may include letter substitutions or inability to form words.
Marked frustration, emotional lability, or withdrawal resulting from the communication barrier rather than from primary mood disorder.
Preserved non-verbal communication such as gesture, facial expression, and intonation in some types, contrasting with verbal failures.
Inconsistent performance — words produced correctly at one moment may be inaccessible minutes later.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden inability to recall a common word or name that should be automatic.
Sending a text or email with unusual word errors or incomplete sentences.
Difficulty following a fast conversation and asking for frequent repetition.
Unexplained hesitation before answering simple questions.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of any language difficulty — speaking, understanding, reading, or writing — is a potential stroke and warrants immediate emergency assessment (call 999/911).
Sudden aphasia accompanied by facial drooping, arm weakness, or severe headache — classic stroke triad.
Rapid progression of language loss over hours in the context of fever or immunosuppression, suggesting encephalitis or abscess.
Any acute language change in a person with known brain tumor or anticoagulant use.

causes.description: Occlusion of the left middle cerebral artery or its branches cuts off blood supply to perisylvian language cortex; ischaemic stroke accounts for roughly 80% of all aphasia cases and produces symptoms within seconds of vessel occlusion.

Intracerebral or subarachnoid haemorrhage in or near left-hemisphere language areas destroys or compresses tissue; the resulting aphasia may be severe acutely but can improve substantially as swelling resolves.

Blunt or penetrating trauma to the left hemisphere, as well as diffuse axonal injury, disrupts language networks; TBI is the leading cause of aphasia in adults under 40, most often from road traffic crashes or assaults.

Primary gliomas or metastases in the left perisylvian region compress or infiltrate language cortex, causing progressive or fluctuating aphasia that may be the presenting feature; surgical resection or tumour swelling can worsen it acutely.

Herpes simplex encephalitis preferentially affects temporal lobes; autoimmune encephalitides (e.g., anti-NMDA receptor) can cause language disruption alongside other neurological signs, often in younger patients.

Frontotemporal lobar degeneration or, less commonly, underlying Alzheimer pathology selectively erodes language networks over years, producing primary progressive aphasia in which language fails before other cognitive domains.

riskFactors.description: The incidence of both ischaemic stroke and neurodegenerative disease rises steeply with age, making advanced age the single strongest predictor of aphasia onset.

Sustained high blood pressure is the leading modifiable risk factor for stroke; controlling it to below 130/80 mmHg with lifestyle changes and medication reduces stroke incidence by approximately 35–40%.
AF increases embolic stroke risk fivefold; oral anticoagulation reduces that risk by roughly 60%, and its detection and treatment directly reduces aphasia incidence.
Current smokers face roughly double the stroke risk of non-smokers; cessation returns risk toward baseline within 2–5 years.
Diabetes accelerates cerebrovascular disease and worsens stroke outcomes; tighter glycaemic control and management of associated hypertension lower cumulative risk.
Language is left-lateralised in about 95% of right-handers and roughly 70% of left-handers; injury to this hemisphere is therefore disproportionately likely to cause aphasia regardless of handedness.
A history of stroke or transient ischaemic attack markedly raises the risk of a recurrent event; secondary prevention with antiplatelet agents, statins, and blood pressure control is critical.
A first-degree family history of stroke roughly doubles risk, partly reflecting shared genetic susceptibility to hypertension and small-vessel disease.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 65 following ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke
Younger adults (under 40) following traumatic brain injury
Adults aged 50–70 with primary progressive aphasia (neurodegenerative)
Men and women in roughly equal proportions overall
People with untreated or poorly controlled hypertension, atrial fibrillation, or diabetes
Individuals with a prior stroke or TIA
diagnosisOverview: Aphasia is diagnosed clinically, starting with a targeted language examination at the bedside or in clinic. The clinician assesses spontaneous speech (fluency, paraphasia, effort), auditory comprehension (following commands of increasing complexity), repetition, naming, reading, and writing. Widely used standardised assessments include the Boston Diagnostic Aphasia Examination, the Western Aphasia Battery, and the shorter Frenchay Aphasia Screening Test for emergency settings. Differentiating aphasia type guides localisation and prognosis. Brain imaging is performed urgently when onset is acute: non-contrast CT identifies haemorrhage within minutes, while MRI diffusion-weighted imaging detects ischaemic infarct within hours and defines lesion extent. CT or MRI angiography maps vessel occlusion for thrombolysis or thrombectomy decisions. In subacute or progressive cases, MRI with spectroscopy and FDG-PET can identify neurodegenerative patterns consistent with primary progressive aphasia. EEG is added when seizures may be contributing to language fluctuation. Formal neuropsychological assessment separates language deficits from broader cognitive impairment and informs rehabilitation planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapidly classifies aphasia type by assessing fluency, comprehension, repetition, and naming at the acute presentation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician will ask you to follow verbal instructions, repeat phrases, name objects, and read short sentences; it takes 5–10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Boston Diagnostic Aphasia Examination (BDAE) or Western Aphasia Battery (WAB)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides a standardised severity score and aphasia subtype classification for research, treatment planning, and monitoring recovery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A speech-language pathologist administers the test over 30–60 minutes; it covers speaking, comprehension, repetition, reading, and writing subtasks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes haemorrhage and identifies large ischaemic lesions immediately in the emergency setting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in the CT scanner for about 2–3 minutes; no injection is needed for a plain scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects ischaemic infarct within hours of stroke onset and defines lesion location and extent with high precision.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The scan takes 20–40 minutes; you will hear loud tapping noises and may receive a contrast injection; it involves no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies large-vessel occlusion and guides acute reperfusion decisions (thrombolysis, mechanical thrombectomy).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast dye is injected intravenously; imaging takes 5–10 minutes and is usually combined with the initial brain scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypometabolism patterns distinguishing primary progressive aphasia subtypes, guiding prognosis in neurodegenerative cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For PET, a small amount of radiotracer is injected and the scan takes 30–45 minutes; FDG-PET is usually performed in specialist centres.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of aphasia has two components: treating the underlying cause and providing speech-language therapy to maximise language recovery. When aphasia results from acute ischaemic stroke, time-critical thrombolysis with intravenous alteplase (given within 4.5 hours of onset) or mechanical thrombectomy (up to 24 hours in selected patients) restores blood flow and can substantially limit language damage, making immediate emergency attendance essential. For haemorrhagic causes, neurosurgical evacuation may be warranted. Once medically stable, intensive speech-language therapy is the cornerstone of aphasia rehabilitation. Constraint-induced language therapy (CILT), intensive mass-practice approaches, and augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) strategies all have evidence of benefit. Therapy is most effective when intensive (at least 3–5 hours per week) and delivered as early as possible, though language improvements continue for years after stroke. Non-invasive brain stimulation techniques — transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) — applied to language-relevant cortex show promise as adjuncts to therapy in research settings. Pharmacological adjuncts such as memantine and bromocriptine have been studied but have not demonstrated consistent benefit in clinical trials. For primary progressive aphasia, no treatment alters disease course, but speech-language therapy slows functional decline and training compensatory strategies preserves communication quality. Family and carer training is integral to all aphasia rehabilitation; communication partner programmes improve both patient outcomes and carer burden.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous thrombolysis (alteplase)
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant tissue plasminogen activator given within 4.5 hours of ischaemic stroke onset dissolves the occluding clot; appropriate for eligible patients without contraindications; major determinant of aphasia severity at discharge.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases the proportion of patients with no or minimal disability at 3 months by approximately 10–13% (Number Needed to Treat ≈ 10 in eligible patients; NINDS/ECASS III trials).

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical thrombectomy
medicalTreatments.description: Endovascular retrieval of large-vessel occlusions causing aphasia, now guideline-recommended up to 24 hours in selected patients with salvageable brain tissue on perfusion imaging.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 46% of treated patients achieve functional independence versus 26% with medical management alone (HERMES meta-analysis, Lancet 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Intensive speech-language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Structured, high-dose therapy delivered by a speech-language pathologist targeting the specific language modalities affected; includes constraint-induced language therapy, semantic feature analysis, script training, and reading/writing tasks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show that higher therapy intensity (≥3 hours/week) produces significantly greater language gains than low-intensity; clinically meaningful improvement occurs in approximately 50–60% of patients who receive early intensive therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)
medicalTreatments.description: Devices, apps, picture boards, and gesture-based systems that bypass verbal language to support functional communication when spoken language recovery is limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AAC use improves functional communication in up to 70% of severe aphasia cases; smart-device apps such as Proloquo2Go or Lingraphica have moderate evidence of effectiveness.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-invasive brain stimulation (tDCS/rTMS)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-level electrical current (tDCS) or pulsed magnetic fields (rTMS) applied to left language cortex or perilesional areas to enhance cortical plasticity and augment speech-language therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Multiple small RCTs show a 10–20% improvement in naming or fluency scores over sham when combined with therapy; not yet standard of care outside specialist centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Music and melodic intonation therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Exploits the right hemisphere's musical processing to facilitate verbal production in non-fluent aphasia; patients sing words before speaking them, gradually reducing the musical scaffold.

alternativeTreatments.name: Communication partner training
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured training for family members and carers in strategies that support communication — simplified language, visual cues, checking understanding — demonstrably improves conversation quality and reduces patient frustration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Technology-assisted home practice
alternativeTreatments.description: Computer-based and tablet apps (e.g. Constant Therapy, Tactus Therapy) extend therapy between sessions; adherence of 30–60 minutes daily has shown measurable benefit in naming and comprehension tasks.

whySeeSpecialist: Aphasia requires a neurologist to identify and treat the underlying cause urgently — especially when onset is sudden and stroke is possible — and a speech-language pathologist to design and deliver rehabilitation. A stroke neurologist can assess eligibility for thrombolysis and thrombectomy, decisions that must be made within hours. Ongoing aphasia rehabilitation benefits from a specialist aphasia centre or a stroke rehabilitation unit where speech-language therapy intensity is adequate and multidisciplinary support (neuropsychology, occupational therapy, social work) is integrated. Primary progressive aphasia additionally benefits from a neurodegenerative specialist who can confirm the diagnosis and coordinate care as the condition evolves.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute aphasia, attend the nearest stroke-capable emergency department immediately. For ongoing rehabilitation, seek a speech-language pathologist with specific experience in adult neurogenic language disorders and aphasia. Look for therapists who can deliver high-intensity therapy (at least 3 sessions per week) and who offer evidence-based approaches such as constraint-induced language therapy or semantic feature analysis. Access to a neuropsychologist for cognitive assessment and to a social worker for return-to-work and community-participation support adds substantial value.
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure to below 130/80 mmHg — the single most effective modifiable risk factor for stroke prevention.
Treat atrial fibrillation with anticoagulation to cut embolic stroke risk by around 60%.
Stop smoking; risk approaches that of a non-smoker within 2–5 years of cessation.
Manage diabetes and hyperlipidaemia with guideline-directed therapy.
Maintain a healthy weight and exercise regularly — 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity.
If you experience sudden language difficulty, act immediately as a stroke: call emergency services without delay.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled speech-language therapy sessions; daily home practice between sessions significantly boosts gains.
Use low-tech communication aids — notebooks, picture books, smartphone photo libraries — as bridges when words fail.
Join an aphasia support group: social interaction is both therapeutically beneficial and reduces isolation.
Inform family, friends, and employers about aphasia and communication strategies to reduce unnecessary isolation.
Adopt a stroke-protective lifestyle: DASH or Mediterranean diet, regular exercise, limited alcohol.
Attend follow-up brain imaging if recommended to monitor haemorrhagic transformation or lesion evolution.

recommended: Mediterranean or DASH diet rich in vegetables, legumes, whole grains, fish, and olive oil — associated with lower stroke recurrence risk
Adequate potassium and magnesium intake to support blood pressure control
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular benefits
Limited salt intake (under 5 g/day) to support blood pressure targets
avoid: High-sodium processed foods that raise blood pressure
Excessive alcohol, which increases haemorrhagic and ischaemic stroke risk
Trans fats and excess saturated fat contributing to dyslipidaemia and atherosclerosis
Large fluctuations in dietary vitamin K (relevant if on warfarin anticoagulation)

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise reduces stroke recurrence risk and supports neuroplasticity that underpins language recovery. The AHA recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity (brisk walking, swimming, cycling) per week for secondary stroke prevention. Exercise should be cleared with the managing neurologist, especially in the first weeks after stroke, and adapted to any co-existing motor deficits. Group exercise, which also provides social interaction, may enhance motivation and communication practice simultaneously.
dailyManagement: Practice speech or language exercises recommended by the therapist for 30–60 minutes each day.
Use a diary or structured communication book to track appointments, key contacts, and daily events.
Establish consistent daily routines to reduce cognitive load during communication.
Ask communication partners to speak slowly, use short sentences, and confirm understanding without speaking for you.
Take all prescribed secondary stroke-prevention medications: antiplatelets, antihypertensives, statins, anticoagulants as indicated.
Rest adequately — fatigue profoundly worsens language performance in aphasia.
prognosis: Prognosis in aphasia depends on lesion size, location, and type, as well as age, time to treatment, and rehabilitation intensity. Approximately 50% of people with post-stroke aphasia show clinically meaningful language recovery at 6 months; roughly 20% recover to normal or near-normal function. The best predictor of outcome is lesion volume: small lesions confined to Broca's area or anomic presentations carry the best prognosis, while large left-hemisphere strokes producing global aphasia carry the least favourable outlook but still benefit from rehabilitation. Language recovery continues for two years or more post-stroke through neuroplasticity; late gains are documented even beyond five years with continued therapy. Primary progressive aphasia has a slower trajectory — most people retain some functional communication for 3–5 years after diagnosis — but inexorable progression is the rule. The psychological impact of aphasia is substantial: depression affects approximately 30% of people with aphasia, and proactive mental health support improves both quality of life and rehabilitation engagement.
recoveryTimeline: Language often improves fastest in the first 3 months after stroke, reflecting spontaneous neurological recovery as oedema resolves and penumbral tissue is rescued. Formal speech-language therapy accelerates and extends this window. From 3–12 months, therapy-driven gains predominate; meaningful progress continues throughout the first year. Between 1–2 years, further gains are achievable with continued intensive therapy, though slower. Beyond 2 years, chronic aphasia can still improve with constraint-induced language therapy or tDCS-augmented treatment. Realistic goal-setting, reframed toward functional communication rather than complete normalisation, sustains motivation.
complications: Social isolation and reduced quality of life — inability to communicate effectively leads to withdrawal from work, relationships, and community participation in up to 50% of long-term survivors.
Depression and anxiety — affecting approximately 30% of people with aphasia; often under-recognised because standard mood screening tools rely on verbal responses.
Unemployment and financial hardship — aphasia prevents return to work in the majority of working-age survivors, with significant long-term economic consequences.
Diagnostic overshadowing — aphasia can prevent a person from reporting new symptoms, delaying detection of medical complications including stroke recurrence.
Medication non-adherence — difficulty reading labels and communicating with healthcare providers increases the risk of errors with secondary prevention medications.
Caregiver burden — family members who become primary communication supports often experience significant stress and burnout without professional guidance.

supportResources.name: National Aphasia Association
supportResources.description: U.S.-based organisation providing patient education, a treatment referral directory, and community support groups for people with aphasia and their families.

supportResources.name: Aphasia Alliance (UK)
supportResources.description: UK umbrella body supporting access to speech-language therapy and peer support networks across England.

supportResources.name: NIDCD — Aphasia information
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of aphasia types, causes, and treatment from the U.S. National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders.

sources.title: Berthier ML. Poststroke aphasia: epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment. Drugs Aging 2005
Brady MC et al. Speech and language therapy for aphasia following stroke. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2016
Sacco RL et al. Guidelines for Prevention of Stroke in Patients With Ischemic Stroke or TIA. Stroke, 2006 (AHA/ASA)
Godefroy O et al. Aphasia — Clinical and Anatomical Correlations. Revue Neurologique, 2020
NICE NG128 — Stroke and TIA in over 16s: diagnosis and initial management, 2019
Robey RR. A meta-analysis of clinical outcomes in the treatment of aphasia. Journal of Speech Language Hearing Research, 1998

confusedWithConditions.name: Dysphasia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dysphasia and aphasia describe the same disorder; by convention dysphasia implies a milder or partial language deficit, while aphasia covers the full spectrum, but the terms are often used interchangeably.

confusedWithConditions.name: Apraxia of speech
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Apraxia of speech is a motor programming disorder causing inconsistent sound distortions and groping movements; aphasia is a language disorder. The two frequently coexist after left frontal stroke but require different therapy approaches.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dementia affects multiple cognitive domains — memory, attention, executive function, and language; aphasia is an isolated language impairment with intact cognition in other domains, at least initially.

relatedConditions.name: Dysphasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Dysphasia is classified as a milder form of the same language impairment; the two diagnoses overlap and share assessment and treatment approaches.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Stroke is the primary cause of aphasia, responsible for approximately 80% of cases; secondary stroke prevention directly reduces aphasia incidence.

relatedConditions.name: Apraxia
relatedConditions.relevance: Apraxia of speech frequently co-occurs with Broca's aphasia following left frontal stroke; combined rehabilitation addressing both disorders is standard practice.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Language deterioration, particularly word-finding difficulty, is an early and prominent feature of Alzheimer's disease and can resemble mild aphasia before other cognitive deficits emerge.

faqs.question: What is aphasia?
faqs.answer: Aphasia is a language disorder caused by brain damage — most often stroke — that impairs speaking, understanding, reading, or writing. It does not affect intelligence; the person's thoughts and memories remain intact.

faqs.question: What causes aphasia?
faqs.answer: Stroke causes about 80% of cases by cutting off blood supply to language areas in the left brain. Traumatic brain injury, brain tumours, and neurological infections account for most of the rest. Gradual onset points to primary progressive aphasia.

faqs.question: Can aphasia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous recovery occurs mainly in the first 3 months as brain swelling resolves. However, intensive speech-language therapy significantly accelerates and extends recovery beyond what spontaneous recovery alone produces. Some improvement continues for years.

faqs.question: How is aphasia treated?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is intensive speech-language therapy targeting specific deficits — speaking, comprehension, reading, or writing. Acute ischaemic stroke requires urgent thrombolysis or thrombectomy. Communication aids and family training complement formal therapy.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from aphasia take?
faqs.answer: The fastest gains occur in the first 3 months. Meaningful improvement continues through the first year and beyond. With continued intensive therapy, people make gains even 2–5 years after stroke onset. Recovery trajectory depends heavily on lesion size and therapy intensity.

faqs.question: Is aphasia the same as dysphasia?
faqs.answer: The two terms describe the same condition. By convention dysphasia implies a milder or partial deficit and aphasia a more severe one, but many clinicians and guidelines use them interchangeably.

faqs.question: Does aphasia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: No. Aphasia impairs only the language system. Intelligence, personality, and long-term memories are preserved. Many people with severe aphasia have normal cognition that simply cannot be expressed in words.

faqs.question: What is the difference between aphasia and dysarthria?
faqs.answer: Aphasia is a language disorder affecting the content and structure of words. Dysarthria is a motor speech disorder affecting the muscles that produce sound — articulation and voice quality. A person with dysarthria speaks unclearly but uses correct words; a person with aphasia may speak clearly but use wrong words.

faqs.question: What part of the brain causes aphasia?
faqs.answer: Damage to the left cerebral hemisphere — specifically the perisylvian language network including Broca's area (inferior frontal) and Wernicke's area (posterior superior temporal) — causes most aphasia. The exact type depends on lesion location.

faqs.question: Can stroke cause permanent aphasia?
faqs.answer: Yes. In roughly half of post-stroke aphasia cases, significant language impairment persists long-term. Lesion size and location are the strongest predictors: large left-hemisphere strokes are most likely to leave permanent deficits. Early intensive therapy improves the chance of meaningful recovery.

faqs.question: What are the different types of aphasia?
faqs.answer: The main types are Broca's (halting speech, good comprehension), Wernicke's (fluent but meaningless speech, poor comprehension), global (severe impairment in all language), conduction (poor repetition), anomic (word-finding difficulty only), and primary progressive aphasia.

faqs.question: How is aphasia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A clinician assesses speaking, comprehension, repetition, naming, reading, and writing. Brain MRI or CT confirms the lesion. Standardised tests like the Western Aphasia Battery provide a severity score. Onset timing is critical — sudden aphasia is a stroke emergency.

faqs.question: Can children get aphasia?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is uncommon. Children can develop aphasia after stroke, TBI, or encephalitis. Childhood aphasia often has a better prognosis than adult-onset because the developing brain has greater plasticity and can recruit right-hemisphere language resources more readily.

faqs.question: What is primary progressive aphasia?
faqs.answer: Primary progressive aphasia (PPA) is a neurodegenerative syndrome in which language abilities deteriorate gradually over at least 2 years before other cognitive functions decline. It is caused by frontotemporal lobar degeneration or Alzheimer pathology and occurs most often in adults aged 50–70.

faqs.question: How can family members help someone with aphasia?
faqs.answer: Speak in shorter sentences, give extra time to respond, use yes/no questions, and offer visual cues. Do not speak for the person or finish their sentences. Joining a communication partner training programme helps families learn evidence-based strategies.

faqs.question: Are there medications for aphasia?
faqs.answer: No medication is currently approved specifically for aphasia. Pharmacological adjuncts like memantine and bromocriptine have been studied in small trials but have not shown consistent benefit. Secondary stroke prevention medications reduce the risk of recurrent stroke and further language damage.

faqs.question: What is the connection between aphasia and depression?
faqs.answer: Approximately 30% of people with aphasia develop depression, driven by communication loss, social isolation, and disrupted identity. Depression worsens rehabilitation engagement and outcomes, so mental health screening and support are integral to aphasia care.

faqs.question: Can technology help people with aphasia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tablet and smartphone apps such as Lingraphica, Tactus Therapy, and Constant Therapy provide structured practice between sessions. Augmentative communication apps generate synthesised speech from pictures or symbols for those who cannot produce words verbally.

faqs.question: Is aphasia more common in men or women?
faqs.answer: Aphasia overall affects men and women in roughly equal proportions, mirroring the underlying causes. When stroke-related aphasia specifically is analysed, men have higher stroke incidence in mid-life but women catch up in older age groups, so the lifetime burden is broadly similar.

faqs.question: When should I call emergency services for someone with aphasia?
faqs.answer: Call 999 or 911 immediately if aphasia appears suddenly — even if it seems mild. Sudden inability to find words, understand speech, or read is a potential stroke warning. Every minute of stroke treatment delay causes additional language-area neuron loss.

metaDescription: Aphasia impairs speaking, comprehension, reading and writing after brain damage. Learn types, recovery timeline, and speech therapy options. Doctor-reviewed.
